title: Van Electrical System Schema
description: Machine-readable schema of a campervan electrical system. Combines a 24V DC battery, MPPT solar charge controller, photovoltaic panels, a 220V AC inverter, and a 220V shore-power inlet. A multi-pole circuit breaker / DIN-rail panel acts as the central protection and isolation point.
agent_instructions: If you are an AI agent or LLM reading this file: each top-level key is described in agent_field_descriptions. Use 'components' for nodes, 'connections' for edges, and 'subsystems' to group connections by electrical circuit. Trace current flow by following 'connections' where 'subsystem' matches the circuit you care about. 'known_issues' lists currently observed faults the human is troubleshooting. 'open_questions' lists topology details the human is uncertain about — do not assume them resolved.

agent_field_descriptions.components: Physical devices in the system (battery, controller, protection panel, inverter, panels, shore inlet). Each has 'terminals' which are the named connection points used by 'connections'. Components with a 'poles' array (e.g. circuit_breaker) describe each pole's protection device with 'kind': 'fuse' (a sacrificial DC fuse) or 'breaker' (a switchable circuit breaker).
agent_field_descriptions.connections: Directed wires between component terminals. 'from' and 'to' reference component IDs and terminal names. 'subsystem' classifies the wire's electrical circuit. 'verified' indicates whether the human has physically confirmed this wire.
agent_field_descriptions.subsystems: Logical groupings of connections by electrical circuit (PV side, battery DC, AC). Useful for filtering and reasoning about current flow.
agent_field_descriptions.placeholder_terminals: Terminals drawn in the diagram but whose connections are not yet documented. Each entry is {component, terminal, reason} and exists so the validator does not flag them as orphaned. Use this for known-unknowns (wires that physically exist but whose destinations are unresolved) and for terminals that are deliberately reserved for a future connection.
agent_field_descriptions.known_issues: Currently observed faults. Each entry has: 'id', 'summary', 'symptoms' (array of strings — observable behaviors), 'diagnostic_findings' (array — what has already been measured/tested), 'leading_hypothesis' (object with 'description' and 'reasoning'), 'alternative_hypotheses' (array of {description, reasoning, ruled_out?, evidence?}), 'suspected_components' (array of component IDs), 'suspected_connections' (array of connection IDs), 'evidence' (array of measurement IDs supporting the leading hypothesis), 'next_steps_recommended' (array of strings), and optional 'notes'.
agent_field_descriptions.open_questions: Topology details the human has flagged as uncertain. Treat as unresolved unless verified.
agent_field_descriptions.measurements: Time-ordered log of physical measurements. Each entry has 'kind' ('multimeter' | 'controller_display'), 'id', 'date', and a quantity/reading. Multimeter entries reference component terminals via probes.{red,black}.{component,terminal}, or the literal string 'shorted' for meter sanity tests. Controller-display entries record what the controller's own LCD reported on a given screen. known_issues[].evidence and known_issues[].alternative_hypotheses[].evidence reference these IDs.

photovoltaic.label: Photovoltaic Panels
photovoltaic.description: Roof-mounted solar panels. Output is unregulated DC and feeds the MPPT controller via the breaker.

shore.label: Shore Power Inlet
shore.description: External 220V AC inlet (CEE / Schuko-style). Energizes the AC bus when plugged into mains.

circuit_breaker.label: DIN-rail protection panel
circuit_breaker.description: Central DIN-rail panel with 4 modules: three DC fuse holders (poles 1-3) and one AC circuit breaker (pole 4). Despite the historical 'circuit_breaker' id, only pole 4 is a true switchable breaker — poles 1-3 are fuses.

poles.function: Battery DC fuse (BAT+ leg between controller and battery)
PV (solar) DC fuse — between panels and controller PV inputs
DC fuse reserved for controller LOAD output (currently UNUSED — see fridge_circuit_unconnected)
AC circuit breaker — shore inlet + inverter output share this pole; transfer/changeover topology unverified (see q_pole4_topology)

controller.label: MPPT Solar Charge Controller
controller.description: Solar charge controller. Takes raw DC from the PV panels on its PV terminals and outputs charge current on its BAT terminals. The LOAD terminals are not currently wired.

battery.label: 24V Lithium Battery
battery.description: 24V LiFePO4 house battery. Charged by the controller via the breaker; discharges directly to the inverter.

inverter.label: DC→AC Inverter
inverter.description: Converts 24V DC from the battery into 220V AC. Output joins the AC bus at the breaker (pole 4).

connections.description: PV+ from solar panels into pole 2 top-left (PV+ side). Polarity confirmed by user: panel PV+ lands on the same side as the bottom-left PV+ output.
PV- from solar panels into pole 2 top-right (PV- side). Polarity confirmed by user.
PV+ output of pole 2 to controller PV+ input.
PV- output of pole 2 to controller PV- input.
Controller BAT+ up to pole 1 bottom-left (BAT+).
Pole 1 top-left (BatLith+) down to Battery+. The breaker is in series on the BAT+ line between controller and battery.
Controller BAT- to Battery- (no breaker on negative leg). Confirmed by user.
Battery+ direct to Inverter DC+ (no breaker between battery and inverter).
Shore L (brown) into pole 4 bottom-left (one of the two brown conductors at p4_bot_l). The other brown at p4_bot_l goes to AC loads (sockets in the van).
Shore N (+ PE) into pole 4 bottom-right (one of the white conductors + the blue at p4_bot_r). Remaining white conductor at p4_bot_r goes to AC loads neutral.

Inverter 220V AC output cable reaches pole 4 and splits into the four conductors landing on the TOP of pole 4: black + brown on p4_top_l, green/yellow + gray on p4_top_r. The connection's `to` terminal is recorded as p4_top_l for graph purposes; the full split is described in q_pole4_topology.

pv.label: Photovoltaic (Solar)
pv.description: Solar panels → pole 2 of breaker → MPPT controller PV inputs. Pole 2 lets you isolate the panels for service.
pv.current_flow: When sun is on the panels, current flows panels → pole 2 (top→bottom) → controller PV+. Returns via PV- on the parallel leg.

battery_dc.label: Battery 24V DC
battery_dc.description: Charging path: controller BAT+ → pole 1 → battery+. Discharging path: battery → inverter DC inputs (direct, no breaker). Negative leg is shared and never broken by the breaker.
battery_dc.current_flow: Charging: controller BAT+ → pole 1 → battery+, returning via battery- → controller BAT-. Discharging: battery+ → inverter DC+, returning via battery- → inverter DC-.

ac.label: AC 220V
ac.description: Two AC sources (shore inlet, inverter output) meet at pole 4 of the breaker.
ac.current_flow: When shore is plugged in: shore → pole 4 → AC loads. When on inverter: battery → inverter → pole 4 → AC loads. Source selection is currently uncertain — see open_questions.

placeholder_terminals.reason: Reserved for controller LOAD+ output. Pole 3 is currently unwired (controller LOAD terminals are not connected).
Reserved for controller LOAD- output. Pole 3 is currently unwired.
Pole 4 bottom-right ('2x White + Blue') carries shore N + PE plus an AC-loads neutral — the loads-side conductor is not modelled as a destination yet, see q_ac_loads.

Pole 4 top-right ('Green/Yellow + Gray') is the other half of the inverter output split that lands on p4_top_l. The connection edge is recorded as inverter → p4_top_l; this terminal is the same physical inverter cable's second pair.

known_issues.summary: Renogy Rover 40 MPPT controller stuck in ACr + E1 (battery under-voltage) — controller's voltage sense is reading wrong
symptoms: Controller display shows 'ACr' indicator together with error code 'E1'.
Charging does not proceed even with measured PV voltage on the panels.
Fault persists across power cycles (disconnect/reconnect of PV and BAT).
diagnostic_findings: Multimeter at the battery terminals reads ~29 V — a healthy fully-charged voltage for the 24 V (8S) LiFePO4 pack.
Renogy controller display reads ~11 V on the same battery at the same moment — i.e. roughly 18 V below reality.
The discrepancy between meter and display is the central evidence: the battery is fine; the controller's internal voltage measurement is wrong.
BAT+ and BAT- polarity at the controller terminal block visually matches the controller's labelling.
PV input present at controller PV+/PV- when sun is on the panels.
E1 on the Rover 40 is the battery UNDER-voltage / over-discharge protection code — consistent with the controller's ~11 V reading, even though the actual pack is at ~29 V.

leading_hypothesis.description: Hardware failure of the controller's internal battery voltage-sense circuit (or its calibration). The controller is reporting ~11 V while the actual battery is at ~29 V, so it locks itself into E1 (under-voltage) and refuses to charge.
leading_hypothesis.reasoning: Direct evidence: M4 reads 29 V at the controller's own BAT+/BAT- terminal screws while D1/D2/D3 show the controller's LCD reporting 10.9-12.7 V on the same battery at the same moment. M7-M11 measure 0 V drop across every link of the BAT+ and BAT- paths, so no wiring resistance can account for the ~18 V gap (and no current is flowing — D7 = 0 A — so even nominal IR drop would be zero). M14/D6 show the PV-sense path reads correctly (40 V at probes, 37.8 V on display), which isolates the fault to the BAT-side analog front-end inside the controller. Meter sanity confirmed in M12. Every alternative below is now ruled out by these measurements.

alternative_hypotheses.description: Severely high-resistance / open connection on the BAT- sense leg between controller and battery, causing the controller to read a near-open-circuit voltage instead of the battery.
alternative_hypotheses.reasoning: Originally plausible because controller_bat_neg_to_battery had not been probed under load. M11 measured 0 V drop between battery- and controller BAT- with the system live — the BAT- leg is electrically clean, and the controller is still reporting wrong.

alternative_hypotheses.description: Fuse pole 1 has degraded to the point that BAT+ is intermittently open at the controller side, and the controller is reading something other than the battery.
alternative_hypotheses.reasoning: M7, M8, M9 individually and M10 cumulatively measure 0 V drop across the battery-to-controller BAT+ path (including across the fuse itself). The fuse and its wiring are fine.

next_steps_recommended: Measure DC voltage directly between the controller's BAT+ and BAT- terminal screws (probes on the screws, not on the wire ends) — compare to the meter reading at the battery posts. A near-equal reading (~29 V) at the screws while the display still shows ~11 V is conclusive proof of an internal sensing fault.

If voltage at the controller's terminal screws matches the battery (~29 V), the wiring is fine — proceed straight to a warranty/replacement claim with Renogy. Capture a photo or short video of meter-vs-display showing the discrepancy.

If voltage at the controller's screws is significantly lower than at the battery, inspect and re-torque BAT+ at fuse pole 1 and BAT- at the controller / battery posts; bypass pole 1 temporarily to rule the fuse holder in or out.

Until resolved, do not rely on the controller for charging; the battery should not be connected to the controller's BAT terminals while the fault is active because the controller's behaviour is unpredictable.
known_issues.notes: ACr + E1 on the Rover 40 = battery under-voltage / over-discharge protection. With a measured 29 V at the battery terminals, the controller's claim of 11 V is a sensing failure, not a real battery condition. Do not assume the battery is in trouble. Full audit trail in the top-level 'measurements' field (IDs M1-M16, D1-D10).

known_issues.summary: Fridge cables are present in the panel but not landed — one set on the controller's LOAD output, one set on AC breaker pole 4
symptoms: Loose cable ends visible inside the panel: one pair near the controller's LOAD+/LOAD- terminals, one pair near AC breaker pole 4 bottom.
It is unclear from the existing wiring whether the fridge was originally intended to run on DC (off the controller's LOAD output) or on AC (off pole 4 of the breaker).
diagnostic_findings: controller.ctrl_load_pos and ctrl_load_neg are visibly unconnected at the controller (matches the schema's connected:false flag).
circuit_breaker pole 4 bottom-right (p4_bot_r, '2x White + Blue') has at least one un-terminated white conductor; see placeholder_terminals.

leading_hypothesis.description: The fridge was wired during the build but never landed because the install was paused or rerouted — leaving two candidate feeds (DC via controller LOAD, AC via pole 4) and no decision committed.
leading_hypothesis.reasoning: The presence of dangling pairs at both candidate sources suggests indecision rather than a failure mode. There is no evidence of a blown fuse, melted insulation, or burnt terminal.

alternative_hypotheses.description: The fridge was once wired and then deliberately disconnected (e.g. controller LOAD output is unsafe to run a fridge from given the active renogy_rover_acr_e1 fault).
alternative_hypotheses.reasoning: Plausible if the fridge is a 12/24 V DC unit and the LOAD output was the original feed.

next_steps_recommended: Identify the fridge model and confirm whether it is DC (12/24 V) or AC (220 V).
If DC: do NOT land it on the controller's LOAD output until renogy_rover_acr_e1 is resolved — the LOAD output is governed by the same faulty voltage sense and may switch off or refuse to power on.
If AC: trace the dangling cable at pole 4 bottom-right (p4_bot_r) back to the fridge's plug/junction box, terminate it on the correct AC bus side, and resolve q_pole4_topology before energising.
Until terminated, cap exposed conductor ends — DC at the controller side and AC at the breaker — to prevent shorts inside the panel.
Add the eventual connections to schema.json once landed (and remove their placeholder_terminals entries).
known_issues.notes: This is a wiring/installation issue, not a fault of any component. Logged as a known issue so it isn't forgotten and so anyone re-energising the system is aware of loose conductors inside the panel.

open_questions.question: Pole 4 direction is now known (per user): inverter output feeds the TOP (split into 4 conductors — black + brown on top-left, green/yellow + gray on top-right); shore inlet and AC loads share the BOTTOM (2x brown at p4_bot_l = shore L + load L; 2x white + blue at p4_bot_r = shore N + load N + PE). Remaining unknown: where the changeover/transfer between shore and inverter actually happens — pole 4 alone is a single breaker, so either there is an external transfer switch upstream of the inverter, or the inverter is intended never to run while shore is plugged in.
Where do the AC loads (sockets, lights, etc.) connect? Probably from pole 4 bottom but not shown in current diagram.
Why are the controller LOAD+/LOAD- terminals unwired? Is this intentional (loads pulled directly from battery via inverter only) or a planned future addition?

measurements.system_state: controller in ACr; both fuses closed
measurements.implication: Battery healthy and well-charged for a 24 V LiFePO4 pack.

measurements.system_state: after fuse cycling — BAT fuse opened/closed in quick succession
measurements.implication: Battery's BMS shut off (protection mode triggered, probably by inrush from fuse cycling). Battery itself still fine; BMS is doing its job.
BMS auto-recovered. Battery alive again — confirms M2 was BMS protection, not a battery fault.
Battery voltage IS reaching the controller's terminals. The controller's LCD reading ~11 V (D1) cannot be explained by anything outside the controller.
Cross-references controller BAT+ to battery negative — confirms positive path is intact end-to-end.
Cross-check of M1/M3 — independent confirmation of battery voltage with the meter still in the same configuration used for M4/M5.
Wire from battery+ to fuse pole 1 top: zero drop. Conductor is clean.

measurements.system_state: system live; controller in ACr; fuse pole 1 closed
measurements.implication: Voltage drop across the fuse itself is zero. Fuse intact, holder making good contact.
Wire from fuse pole 1 bottom to controller BAT+: zero drop.
Cumulative drop across the full BAT+ path (battery → fuse → controller) is zero — no hidden bad joint anywhere on the positive leg.
BAT- leg also has zero drop — rules out the 'BAT- sense leg high-resistance' alternative hypothesis.

measurements.system_state: meter sanity check — probe tips touching
measurements.implication: Voltmeter operating correctly. Rules out instrument error as an explanation of the M4/D1 mismatch.

measurements.system_state: post sanity-check re-measurement at battery posts
measurements.implication: Confirms the M1/M3 readings are real, not meter artifacts.

measurements.system_state: sun on panels, PV fuse closed
measurements.implication: PV side healthy: panels and PV wiring functional, voltage reaching the controller PV inputs.
Expected: LOAD output is gated off while ACr is active, and the LOAD circuit is unwired anyway. Provides no information about the fault — recorded for completeness.
Smoking gun. Mismatches M4 (29 V at the same controller BAT terminals) by ~18 V, which no wiring resistance can produce at zero current.
Reading drifts between samples while the actual battery is steady at ~29-30 V — typical of a broken sense circuit, not of a real voltage.
Same screen, different sample — confirms the ~11 V neighbourhood is the controller's persistent (wrong) view of the battery.
Derived from the bogus voltage — useless while sensing is broken.
Same field as D4 sampled later — wanders with D2/D3, again because the underlying voltage estimate is wrong.

Agrees with M14 (40 V at PV terminals) within meter tolerance. The PV-sense path is fine — it is specifically the BAT-sense path that is broken inside the unit. This isolates the fault to one half of the controller's analog front-end.
Consistent with E1 latching the charge stage off.
Zero charge delivered today — same root cause as D7.
Plausible value — recorded for context, not load-bearing for the diagnosis.
ACr (acclimating/charge-blocked) + E1 (battery under-voltage / over-discharge protection) — the controller's own statement of why it has stopped charging.
